risks: Vietnam jet fuel cuts now 3 days away: Vietnam Airlines suspending 7 domestic routes and cutting 23 weekly flights from April 1 (Bloomberg Mar 27)
VietJet cancelling Korea-Vietnam flights en masse including Nha Trang routes from April (Seoul Economic Daily Mar 25)
AirAsia has no significant fuel hedging against 47% fuel cost spike since January; absorbing surge in full
Jet A-1 at SEA hubs now $234.3/bbl as of Mar 29, up from $227.4 on Mar 28 -- still climbing
Vietnam imports 60%+ of jet fuel from China and Thailand, both of which have banned exports with no signs of lifting
April 19 departure is within the at-risk window; carriers project schedule restoration only if fuel shipments arrive April 10-15
mitigations: Short-haul route (2hr, 956km) limits fuel exposure per flight
Thai AirAsia still operating CXR-DMK 7x weekly (FD647) as of late March
SEA airspace completely unaffected by Middle East closures
21 days until departure -- still time to book VietJet or Vietnam Airlines CXR-BKK as backup
Bangkok is a critical hub; carriers have strong incentive to maintain CXR-DMK even under reduced schedules

risks: Middle East airspace closures force rerouting: Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Syria FIRs all closed by NOTAM
Expect 90 min to 3 hours additional flight time via southern (Egypt-Saudi-Oman) or northern (Caucasus) corridor
Brent crude at $112.57/bbl (Mar 27-29), Goldman Sachs estimates $14-18 geopolitical risk premium baked in
Iran rejected US peace talks Mar 26, removing near-term hope of airspace reopening before April 27
Oman Air cancelled CPH flights until further notice, indicating European route stress across carriers
mitigations: Thai Airways is a government-backed flag carrier, most resilient carrier on this route
BKK-CPH direct route still operating with rerouting and minimal schedule impact (74 delays, no cancellations)
Southern route (Egypt-Saudi-Oman corridor) is established and currently favored by most carriers
BKK benefiting from rerouted traffic, increasing capacity and infrastructure at Suvarnabhumi
29 days until departure provides buffer for situation changes

fuelOil.summary: Brent crude holding at $112.57/bbl (Mar 27-29), highest since July 2022. Jet A-1 imported from Singapore at $234.3/bbl as of Mar 29, up from $227.4 on Mar 28. Goldman Sachs estimates $14-18/bbl geopolitical risk premium in current prices. Prices have roughly doubled from pre-crisis levels.
details: Jet A-1 at SEA hubs now $234.3/bbl (Mar 29), up from $227.4 on Mar 28 -- still climbing daily
Jet fuel prices rose from ~$95/bbl in late Feb to $234/bbl by late March, roughly 2.5x pre-war levels
China and Thailand fuel export bans still in effect, directly cutting Vietnam's supply with no end date
Vietnam fuel delivery timelines extended from 30 to 50+ days
Oil market in backwardation, signaling continued supply tightness through Q2 2026
Vietnam Airlines planning 10-20% monthly flight cuts next quarter if jet fuel stays above $160-200/bbl
Asia's air travel crisis now risks spreading to Europe per Bloomberg/Japan Times (Mar 27-28)

geopolitical.iranConflict: Active conflict. US-Israeli strikes on Iran Feb 28, 2026 prompted retaliation. Iran rejected direct US peace talks Mar 26 -- no near-term diplomatic resolution. Strait of Hormuz effectively closed to commercial traffic since Mar 2, disrupting ~17.8M bbl/day. No signs of de-escalation.
airspaceClosures: Iran (OIIX FIR) -- CLOSED to commercial aviation
Israel (LLLL FIR) -- RESTRICTED, limited PPR only
Bahrain, UAE, Qatar -- HEAVILY RESTRICTED, short-notice closures possible
geopolitical.regionalStability: Southeast Asia airspace stable and unaffected by direct conflict. However, fuel supply chains severely disrupted. Thailand acting as transit hub for rerouted Europe-Asia traffic. No direct security threat to SEA aviation.
details: Iranian drones struck Nakhchivan airport (Azerbaijan) on Mar 5, first conflict spillover into Caucasus corridor
Traditional Gulf corridor for Europe-Asia flights effectively unavailable indefinitely
Two rerouting corridors in use: Southern (Egypt-Saudi-Oman, +90min) and Northern (Caucasus-Central Asia, +2-3hrs)
AeroThai reports 1,000+ flights cancelled in Thailand alone due to Gulf crisis cumulative
Tourism arrivals to Thailand dropped 9% in early March, 18% decline from Europe/Middle East specifically

airasia.cancellationRate: Elevated. Among hardest-hit carriers in Mar 10 wave (184 cancellations across Asia). No fuel hedging, absorbing full 47% cost spike. Research house suspended stock call citing uncertainty.
airasia.financialHealth: Budget carrier with thin margins, no significant fuel hedging. Every $1/bbl fuel increase reduces earnings ~7%. Fare increases of 26% insufficient to fully offset the fuel shock.
airasia.recentDisruptions: Multiple disruption events Feb-Mar 2026. Philippines AirAsia suspended Manila-HCMC 3x weekly through May. Thai AirAsia still operating CXR-DMK daily but under increasing pressure. Implementing fuel surcharges on key routes.

thaiAirways.cancellationRate: Near normal. 74 delays but managing better than most carriers. Rerouting European services with minimal schedule impact.
thaiAirways.financialHealth: Government-backed flag carrier, post-restructuring. Absorbing fuel costs via 10-15% ticket price increases. Better positioned than budget carriers to weather the crisis.
thaiAirways.recentDisruptions: BKK-CPH operating with rerouting via southern or northern corridor. Some delays at BKK due to increased transit traffic. No route cancellations for BKK-CPH reported.

cxrToDmk.notes: SEA airspace unaffected by Middle East closures. Primary risk is Vietnam's jet fuel shortage, not airspace. Vietnamese airlines coordinated pullback begins April 1 -- AirAsia CXR-DMK route under pressure. If Thai AirAsia reduces frequency, options are VietJet or Vietnam Airlines CXR-BKK. Monitor AirAsia booking portal from April 1 onwards.

bkkToCph.notes: Traditional Gulf corridor unavailable. Thai Airways rerouting via southern (Egypt-Saudi-Oman, +90min, currently favored) or northern (Caucasus/Central Asia, +2-3hrs) corridors. Route still operating but longer and more expensive. No direct Thai Airways BKK-CPH cancellations reported.

actions.action: Check AirAsia CXR-DMK booking status starting April 1 when Vietnamese airline cuts take effect. If FD647 shows cancelled or rescheduled, immediately book VietJet or Vietnam Airlines CXR-BKK as backup. Don't wait -- alternatives will fill fast.

Consider moving Nha Trang departure earlier to April 10-14. Carriers project fuel-driven schedule restoration by April 10-15 only IF shipments arrive. If they don't, cuts will deepen. Earlier departure = more flight options and fewer surprises.
Thai Airways BKK-CPH operating with rerouting -- expect 1.5-3hr longer flight. Confirm booking is still active and check for any schedule change notifications. No need to rebook yet.
If Brent stays above $120 or additional airspace closures occur, evaluate backup routing: BKK to CPH via Istanbul (Turkish Airlines) or Helsinki (Finnair) as alternatives to Thai Airways.

oilPriceTimeline.event: Pre-conflict baseline. Markets calm, IATA forecast $88/bbl for 2026.
Brent drops to $67.4 as US-Iran diplomatic talks ease tensions.
Markets turn bullish anticipating US military action against Iran. Brent trading ~$10 above fair value.
Last day before strikes. Oil at pre-war levels.
US-Israeli strikes on Iranian military targets. War begins. Immediate price spike.
Iranian missile/drone retaliation. Brent surges 10-13% from pre-war.
Iranian drones strike Nakhchivan airport (Azerbaijan). Brent up 15% since war began.
Thailand bans fuel exports. China restricts refined oil exports.
Peak spike. Near halt in Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic. 65% increase from Feb 27.
Partial correction. Still up 37% from pre-war. IATA warns of sudden price challenge.
Brent eases but jet fuel stays elevated due to supply chain lag.
Iran rejects US peace talks. Prices reignite. Jet fuel up 12.6% week-on-week.
Brent closes at highest since July 2022, up 4.2% on day. Bloomberg/Japan Times: Asia air travel crisis risks spreading to Europe.
Brent holds at 4-year highs. Jet fuel ticking up to $234.3/bbl. Vietnamese airline cuts take effect in 3 days. Goldman Sachs: $14-18 geopolitical risk premium in current prices.

cancellationTimeline.details: Normal disruption levels across SEA. Typical daily: 50-100 delays, <20 cancellations region-wide.
1,198 delays + 21 cancellations across Changi, Jakarta, Bali, Manila, Cebu. Pre-war disruptions.
4,216 delays + 62 cancellations. AirAsia alone had 247 delays. Infrastructure strain at SEA hubs.
Disruptions across Thailand, Japan, Indonesia, Vietnam, Philippines, Hong Kong. ~150-200k passengers affected.
1,400+ delays + 93 cancellations. Jakarta logged 104 delays alone, KL reported 470.
WAR BEGINS. 2,513 cancellations + 2,117 delays. Changi: 32 cancelled. BKK: 32 cancelled. Massive wave.
150-300% above normal daily disruption rates. JAL, Thai Airways, Air India, ANA, AirAsia, Lion Air all affected.
328 Thailand flights disrupted. Hundreds of passengers stranded across Asia.
1,043 delays + 184 cancellations across SEA. AirAsia among hardest-hit carriers.
AeroThai reports cumulative 1,000+ flights cancelled in Thailand alone due to Gulf crisis.
65+ international/regional flights cancelled across Indonesia, Malaysia, Hong Kong, Taiwan.
325 delays + 50 cancellations in Thailand. Thai Airways, Bangkok Airways, AirAsia, Emirates affected. + 56 cancelled in Indonesia/Malaysia.
Vietnamese airlines announce coordinated pullback from April 1. Vietnam Airlines suspending 7 domestic routes, cutting 23 weekly flights. VietJet cutting 8%. Restoration projected April 10-15.
Asia air travel crisis risks spreading to Europe per Bloomberg and Japan Times. Fuel crisis tightening further with jet fuel at $234.3/bbl. Vietnamese airline cuts now 3 days from taking effect.

seaAirlineSummary: Southeast Asian aviation under severe and deepening stress. Fuel crisis at new high: Brent $112.57, jet fuel $234.3/bbl (Mar 29). Vietnamese carriers' coordinated April cuts begin in 3 days. Budget carriers face existential fuel economics with no hedges. Flag carriers managing with rerouting and price hikes but margin pressure mounting. Bloomberg warns crisis is spreading beyond Asia. No relief expected until Q2 unless Middle East stabilizes.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Vietnam Airlines
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Cutting 23 weekly flights and suspending 7 domestic routes from April 1. Bloomberg Mar 27: further suspensions likely. Planning 10-20% monthly cuts if jet fuel stays above $160-200/bbl.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Imports 60%+ of jet fuel from China and Thailand, both of which have banned exports. Fuel delivery timelines stretched from 30 to 50+ days.

seaAirlineOverview.name: VietJet Air
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Cancelling Korea-Vietnam flights en masse including Nha Trang routes from April. Reducing 8% on regional network as part of coordinated Vietnamese pullback.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Budget model makes them highly vulnerable to 47% fuel cost spike. Connects Vietnam to Cambodia, Thailand, Laos.

seaAirlineOverview.name: AirAsia (Group)
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Among hardest-hit in Mar 10 cancellation wave. Philippines AirAsia suspended Manila-HCMC 3x weekly through May. Research house suspended stock call. Affiliate revenue losses exceeding $180M for Q1.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Thin margins, no significant fuel hedging. Thai AirAsia still operating CXR-DMK daily (FD647) but under increasing pressure. Every $1/bbl fuel increase reduces earnings ~7%.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Thai Airways
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Rerouting European services via southern or northern corridor with minimal schedule impact. 74 delays but managing better than most. Raised prices 10-15%.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Government-backed, post-restructuring. Better positioned to absorb fuel costs. BKK-CPH still operating.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Cebu Pacific
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Major suspensions: Davao-BKK, Iloilo-BKK, Iloilo-SGN, Clark-HAN all suspended Apr-Oct 2026. Trimmed frequencies to SIN, JKT, KUL, MEL, SYD.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Philippines declared fuel emergency. Cebu Pacific among most aggressive in cutting routes to survive.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Bangkok Airways
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Implementing fuel surcharges of 1,200-2,000 baht. No major route suspensions reported yet.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Smaller regional carrier. Boutique positioning may help retain premium passengers willing to pay surcharges.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Nok Air
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Implementing fuel surcharges of 1,200-2,000 baht alongside Bangkok Airways. Domestic Thai routes under pressure.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Low-cost subsidiary model. Already operating on thin margins before the crisis.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Lion Air
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Part of broader early-March disruption wave. Indonesian domestic market under fuel pressure.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Limited specific data, but Indonesian carriers facing similar fuel supply constraints.

broaderContext.narrative: The Iran war has triggered a cascading energy crisis across Southeast Asia. Fuel prices have roughly doubled since pre-conflict levels, with jet fuel now at $234.3/bbl (Mar 29). The coordinated Vietnamese airline pullback begins April 1 -- carriers are no longer just reacting to disruptions but planning sustained reductions. Bloomberg and Japan Times now warning the crisis is spreading beyond Asia toward Europe. Iran's rejection of peace talks on Mar 26 removed the last near-term diplomatic off-ramp.

policies.description: PM Order 2/2026: Banned exports of gasoline, gasohol, diesel, Jet A-1 aviation fuel, and LPG (except to Cambodia and Laos). Froze diesel prices. Required large fuel traders (100k+ tonnes/yr) to increase reserves to 1.5-3%.

Diesel shortage crisis deepening. Long queues at fuel stations in provinces. PM denied large-scale hoarding, says everyday users panic-buying (~17M litres/day). Government considering revival of 1973 emergency energy law.
Banned refined fuel exports, including jet fuel. Directly cut off ~60% of Vietnam's jet fuel supply. China prioritizing domestic energy security over regional export commitments.
Declared fuel emergency. Government moved to 4-day work week for government offices. Philippine carriers (Cebu Pacific, Philippines AirAsia) among most aggressive in cutting routes.

Coordinated airline pullback taking effect April 1. Vietnam Airlines suspending 7 domestic routes, cutting 23 weekly flights. VietJet reducing 8%. Officials encouraged to work from home. Fuel delivery timelines 50+ days. Gasoline taxes removed, cutting pump price 19%.
Military junta introduced odds-and-evens driving system (fuel rationing). Alternating driving days based on license plate numbers.

broaderContext.outlook: Aviation disruption will persist through Q2 2026 unless Middle East stability improves. Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed (~17.8M bbl/day disrupted). Iran's rejection of peace talks on Mar 26 removed near-term diplomatic hope. Oil in backwardation signals continued tightness. For SEA, the China/Thailand fuel export bans are the critical chokepoint with no sign of lifting. Vietnamese carriers project partial schedule restoration April 10-15, contingent on fuel shipments arriving. Bloomberg now warning the crisis is spreading to Europe.
